comments.title: Energy dynamics in fiction and reality
comments.content:
Hi JMG, have you seen much evidence that different people have different particular affinities for energy work? Do these affinities follow any kind of regular pattern that you can see?

I was inspired to ask by a Japanese manga series called Hunter X Hunter. In this story, there are characters who develop supernatural abilities through the use of a force called "nen" whose mechanics mirror some real occult concepts. Nen is the control of a person's aura, which is effectively their etheric body. There are a variety of basic skills using the aura, such as controlling its flow, suppressing it to avoid detection or amplifying its output to strengthen the user. The most interesting nen technique is "manifestation," which can produce unique abilities that the user designs based on their personality.

The full details on the ability system are given on this wiki page: https://hunterxhunter.fandom.com/wiki/Nen

The manifested abilities cover a wide gamut from simple to complex and they fall into six categories. Enhancement abilities abilities that boost the user's basic physical attributes. Transmutation abilities change the properties of the user's aura. Materialization abilities generate tangible physical objects from the user's aura. Manipulation abilities use the aura to control physical objects. Emission abilities project the aura away from the body to achieve an effect. Finally, there are "specialization" abilities that do something really unique that doesn't fall into one of the aforementioned categories.

A person is naturally most skilled at one of the above six types and their other talents vary depending on their primary type. For example, someone whose primary skill is enhancement abilities is the least skilled at using materialization and manipulation abilities, because enhancement is a very concrete ability while materialization and manipulation are the most abstract. This is explained in more detail by charts on the wiki page.

The gamut of nen abilities runs from really straightforward ones, like the main character focusing his energy to punch harder, to the obtuse and strange. There is one character named Knuckle who has the ability to conjure a sprite that will attach to an opponent and put them in a state of "aura debt." It does not attack its target and is likewise indestructible, and it bears a digital display on its body that indicates how much "debt" the targeted person is in as a numeric value. It periodically announces the compounding of interest as the number goes up. If the user cannot "pay back their debt" by inflicting an equivalent amount of damage to Knuckle, the debt value will eventually become greater than the target's total aura potential and they will "go bankrupt." The sprite will turn into an imp that clings to the target and forces them into a state of total aura suppression for the next 30 days. A person in this state fighting against nen users is as helpless as a kitten.

The interesting thing about this fictional system is how closely some of its properties hew to real etheric phenomena. One of the basic aura skills is "focusing," gathering aura in a particular part of the body. As an example, characters can gather their aura in their eyes to see other people's auras. I've always been able to readily perceive etheric energy as a dense, electric sensation, and I've found that this technique works in real life to help me view auras.

Of course, you can't actually generate solid matter from your aura, but do you know of people who have developed an affinity for generating particular types of etheric forms? The story postulates that a person's energetic affinities depend on their personality type (https://hunterxhunter.fandom.com/wiki/Nen#Hisoka's_Personality_Test). The six types of nen manifestation seem to mirror some real energetic affinities I've observed, apart perhaps from the overly vague "specialization" category.

Thanks again for these posts.

replies.title: Re: Energy dynamics in fiction and reality
replies.content:
This is going to be a two-part answer. The first part is that, yes, there are differences in talent for etheric work from one person to another. These don't seem to correlate with personality types, whatever might be meant by that very vague term, but they do exist. The rest of the stuff from the game? Slightly less realistic than a game of quidditch.

Second, er, I trust you know this, but the single most effective way to make a fool of yourself on the occult end of the internet is by treating fictional magical systems as a source of real guidance in magic. Many years ago, when dinosaurs walked the earth and Usenet was new, I was tolerably active on alt.magick, and there were already people making fools of themselves by taking ideas from fantasy novels and thinking they were real. Nowadays, with video games and the rest of it, there are many more opportunities to do the same thing, and a lot of people are taking advantage of those.

With this in mind, I'll make a suggestion: in the future, if you want to ask a question about magic, and it's inspired by a video game, leave out the game references. Just ask, say, "Do people differ in their talents for etheric magic?" That way you'll be much more likely to have people take you seriously, and much less likely to have them snicker about you behind your back.

replies.title: Re: Energy dynamics in fiction and reality
replies.content:
Since your first question is about whether people have different affinities for working with subtle energies, I think it's worthwhile pondering why you have come to a particular druid's blog to ask that question. Would you be here in the first place if you were not already aware that certain people have particular affinities for working with subtle energies?

As for fictional systems hewing close to real etheric phenomena, plenty of authors, artists, philosophers, storytellers, scientists, etc. can pick up on various aspects of the subtle realms and then relate those to other people in more or less muddled fashion. Think about J.R.R. Tolkien, Hieronymus Bosch, and Plato, all of whom who were quite competent at embedding their understanding of the unseen in their works. Then think about the enormous money flows that are needed today to get a hit film or video game produced. Even someone with the skill needed to convey through their creations their own understanding of the subtle planes is likely to be required to make a ton of compromises in order to get any small part of their vision into production.

As for patterns that people's affinities tend to fall in, you're likely to have much better luck decoding them if you let go of that game's various conceits and focus on a coherent metaphysical training system instead. Some make an inventory of types of metaphysical aptitudes humans can achieve, such as clairvoyance, astral projection, seeing auras, etc. Some go to great lengths to detail distinctions within the metaphysical realm, such as elements, directions, chakras, planes, the zodiac, etc. If you want to learn to spot repeating patterns in the various affinities that humans can be gifted with in the subtle realms, you could do worse than to hypothesize that each one of them manifests itself through the characteristic energy flows of one of the planes.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Energy dynamics in fiction and reality
replies.content:
To riff off Christophe, it's easy to pick up "conceits" from a variety of sources, not just video games or Hollywood. In my current journey in Christianity, I am encountering phenomena that aren't always mentioned in the Bible, and most certainly not in the popular narrative that is wrapped around Christianity. I have to go with what I personally observe. Whether what I observe is comprehensible to me, that's a whole 'nother 55-gallon barrel of fish. I constantly ask for advice from the known-to-be-benevolent Deity and the known-to-be-benevolent metaphysical being known as Jesus, and they've been most helpful.

Is there 'nen' in this picture? There is certainly something. People call it 'nen', 'chi', 'the force', 'spirit' or whatever, but I see it only through a glass darkly. I suspect that most or all of us see it only through a glass darkly. People's hypotheses, such as this 'HunterXHunter' personality type list, may be useful as jumping-off-points. But test every piece of it; pay closest attention to what you actually experience/perceive (such as the piece where you can gather 'nen' around your eyes and then see other people's auras). Don't cling uncritically to all parts of the hypothesis, be it HunterXHunter or whatever, because that may hobble you or even lead you astray.

Hope this helps,

- Cicada Grove

comments.title: A follow-up question to a 2024 MM reply
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

In response to an August 2024 Magic Monday question, you wrote:

“Incompetent cursing has two karmic dimensions -- the karma of cursing, and the karma of incompetence. The more space you give in your life to hatred, anger, and malice, whether you express it outwardly or not, the more hateful, angry, and malicious you become, and the more people treat you the way they treat hateful, angry, and malicious people -- i.e., not well. Equally, the more tolerant you are of your own incompetence, the more incompetent you become, and that spreads from one area (such as magic) to everything else in life. So these people are setting themselves up for a really miserable future, in which they get to stew in their own nastiness while sinking further and further into failure. The good news is that eventually they'll be miserable enough to do something else.”

I’m here to report that I’m now miserable enough that I want to stop my incompetent cursing and do something else. I believe the karma of incompetence is hitting me like a ton of bricks.

Oddly, I don’t believe the karma of cursing is affecting me as much; my hypothesis is that, although I sometimes do give in to hatred, anger, and malice, I believe I don’t give them “a lot of space” in my life. With that being said, however, what in the beginning (maybe about 28 years ago) was an occasional incompetent curse let out in rare and extremely angry moments, is now as if I have Tourette Syndrome, but my vocalizations are, for the most part, incompetent curses (still triggered by anger, but sometimes the anger is disproportionately small compared to the volume of my voice when I have my “tics”).

- I’m now 45.
- I’ve been in therapy for almost 2 years (after roughly a 2-3 year absence, which was after maybe 4-5 years of continuous therapy, which was after a significant absence, you see the pattern).
- I’ve been a regular attendee at Mass for roughly a year (after essentially a decades-long absence); I actually asked my 2024 Magic Monday question a few days after an almost malevolent encounter at a Costco gas station. It actually prompted me to thank God and attend afternoon Mass that day as a way of thanksgiving.
- I’ve joined a weekly contemplative prayer group last May.
- As part of my Dialectical behavior therapy skills I’m working on, I am working on the Emotion Regulation skill “Build Mastery.”
- I have begun to practice discursive meditation.
- I should have by Monday evening the print edition of the Octagon Society lessons. But I have already started on Preliminary Lesson 1: The Law of Blame (from my point of view, I feel that I started in Aug 2021, when you posted the lessons, but I never followed through).

With all that being said, aside from actually following through with my exercises, is there anything else I can do to reverse/stanch/dam up/burn up the karma of cursing and the karma of incompetence? Or is the following “Babylon 5” quote applicable here?: “The avalanche has already started. It is too late for the pebbles to vote.”

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: A follow-up question to a 2024 MM reply
replies.content: I'm delighted to hear that you took the issue seriously and are doing something about it. You've taken on quite a bit of work; it will take time for that to do the job, but what you're doing is what I would have advised you to do. No, the Babylon 5 quote doesn't apply; the pebbles are your actions, past, present, and future, and you vote by casting them. Which way you cast them, if you do so systematically, can steer the avalanche.

replies.title: Re: A follow-up question to a 2024 MM reply
replies.content:
Oh, my dear, you do not want to stanch or dam up karma, I promise you — it has much more influence than all of us put together! What can help is to let it flow, so that its patterns can resolve and not stay with you through this life or the next.

Helping to reverse or burn up karma is fine and can work wonders, so long as you are allowing karma to flow in those new directions, rather than trying to block it or lock it in place. Blocked, locked, dammed up karma is truly hell on earth. You do not want to go there.

— Christophe

comments.title: 4 questions regarding prayer
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Question 1:
From your knowledge and perspective of the prayers that you’ve encountered, studied, or have second-hand knowledge about, do most prayers follow a basic but formal structural pattern?

I am aware that you co-wrote a book called “Pagan Prayer Beads: Magic and Meditation with Pagan Rosaries,” but there is no eBook edition that I can readily look at.

I can’t remember your exact wording, but I believe you said something like. Prayer is talking to a god.

From my previous perspective, I saw prayer as like reading a letter one has already written to a god. And since it’s a letter, there has to be some formality and structure.

For example, it’s my understanding that one way the Lord’s Prayer can be divided into sections is 1) address to God, 2) petitions addressing God’s concerns regarding humanity, and 3) petitions addressing personal human needs.

However, being highly susceptible to things portrayed in pop culture, I have begun to think of prayers like voicemails to a god, and extremely brief prayers like text messages to a god. The text message imagery is most appealing to me when I’m driving and would like to say a very quick prayer. Which leads me to


Question 2:
From your knowledge and perspective of the different religions that you’ve encountered, studied, or have second-hand knowledge about, can individuals assess the propriety of their personal prayers only by comparing them to the norm in the religion they belong to? Or is simple conviction enough?


Question 3:
I believe you have often said that demons will give the petitioner what he or she asks for, not what he or she wants.

Meanwhile, for Christians, Jesus said: “But when ye pray, use not vain repetitions, as the heathen do: for they think that they shall be heard for their much speaking. Be not ye therefore like unto them: for your Father knoweth what things ye have need of, before ye ask him” (Matthew 6:7-8, King James Version).

I am not sure as to the gods of other religions having omniscience, but I’m assuming so.

From your knowledge and perspective of the different religions that you’ve encountered, studied, or have second-hand knowledge about, are most gods sufficiently omniscient such that any less-than-perfectly-worded prayer is still understood as what the petitioner actually wants when praying?


Question 4:
(somewhat of an aside question regarding the Jesus quote)

Two additional English translations for comparison:
- New International Version: … do not keep on babbling like pagans, for they think they will be heard because of their many words. Do not be like them, for your Father knows what you need before you ask him.
- English Standard Version: … do not heap up empty phrases as the Gentiles do, for they think that they will be heard for their many words.

From how I perceive the 3 different English translations of the quote, and also based on my understanding that, to critics, memorizing the Lord’s Prayer can lead to mindless repetition; I can only think of 2 conclusions, with only 1 of which is correct:
1. Jesus’s description was correct.
2. It was a general (mis)understanding in Jewish teaching at the time that gentile prayers are needlessly long (or choose other disparaging comment), which can be comparable to how Protestants may view the Catholic Rosary as being needlessly long (or choose other disparaging comment).

I know that you aren’t Christian, but I would like to know your opinion on the quote, if you have one.

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: 4 questions regarding prayer
replies.content:
1) Only to the extent that any act of communication has a common structure. Prayers are extremely diverse.

2) The crucial element is paying attention to the response you get. Prayer is as much listening as talking. Say your prayer, and then attend in silence; with a little practice, you'll be able to tell how the prayer is received, and that can guide you in future prayers.

3) That's the great difference between gods and demons. Gods hear what you're thinking and feeling; demons only hear what you're saying.

4) Roman prayers in the time of Jesus were very long, formal, and legalistic -- much more so than, say, Catholic prayers today. My understanding is that that's what Jesus was objecting to: treating prayer as a matter of repeating the proper formulae and expecting a mechanical response. If you want to talk to the gods, talk to them as though they were much older and wiser friends -- that's my take on his advice. And again, listen!

replies.title: Re: 4 questions regarding prayer
replies.content:
(Not OP)
Did those long formal prayers serve as rituals for the petitioner to trace out an old track in space and mechanically "answer" the prayer by their own will?

Patrick

replies.title: Re: 4 questions regarding prayer
replies.content: Good question. Somebody with good clairvoyant skills would have to be present at such a ritual to find out; I don't know of any description of this having happened.

replies.title: Re: 4 questions regarding prayer
replies.content:
I was thinking that these long, rote prayers (purely from the description you gave) seem as "mechanical" as my actions and visualizations when doing the LRP.

I remember Spengler saying that the ancient Romans tried to record all that has been passed down about the gods they honored in ceremonies because in some cases they didn't remember the god's attributes, just kept honoring them out of tradition. Maybe some of those deities never existed, and those ceremonies were rituals to accomplish workings.

I doubt most gods would approve of those self-deceptive magical workings in their names, so these rituals (if that's what they effectively were) probably had high failure rates.

Patrick

replies.title: Re: 4 questions regarding prayer
replies.content:
I think that traditional written prayers like the Lord's Prayer can be extremely useful - IF they are done very slowly, meditatively and mindfully - the prayer acts as a sort of Symbol and your praying it lets you unlock it. I have been praying the Lord's Prayer for decades and I feel like I am barely starting to scratch the surface, and I feel like some of the traditional old Orthodox prayers are powerful enough they should come with a warning label.

Another advantage of mindfully using traditional prayers is that they can take you places that would not occur to you on your own - they stretch consciousness.

I like a mix of set form prayers with freeform, and I often find my own words emerge as a response to something in a set prayer. I use a daily series of memorized prayers as my gateway into a prayerful state of consciousness.

As for the Catholic Rosary - that can be either meaningless repetition or an endless meditative journey depending on how you do it - and extemporaneous prayer can easily become mindless repetition, which is obvious if you have spent any time in groups that use only that.

replies.title: Re: 4 questions regarding prayer
replies.content:
Don't overlook the opportunity to express gratitude in your prayers, rather than just asking for... whatever it is you want to ask for, whether it's peace in our time, preservation of nature, a good used car, etc.

I have found it useful to organize a prayer of thanks to God around: the Earth (and all solid things that make my life possible and comfortable), Water (and all liquids...), Air (and all gases...), and Fire (and all forms of energy...), all things that I appreciate having in my life. Surrounded with such wealth, how could I ask for more? But there are illnesses in some people dear to me, and I can ask for healing and support. So far, so good!

Lathechuck

replies.title: Re: 4 questions regarding prayer
replies.content:
For what it's worth, I also use a mix of set/formal prayers and extemporaneous prayer. In addition to what charlieobert said, all of which I agree with (though in my case, I'm not saying Christian prayers), set/memorized prayers also have the value of reinforcing certain thoughts, symbols, or feelings, so they're good for stuff you want to frequently revisit and might not do so spontaneously. Extemporaneous prayer is, of course, more flexible and great for when you have a specific concern, issue, or request for help.

As for a structure, outside of Christianity (though many Christian prayers roughly follow this pattern as well), documented prayers from different Indo-European-descended peoples tend to follow this rough structure (with variations, of course):

- invocatio: identify which God or Gods to Whom you are praying, whether by name, epithets, characteristics, or whatever
- pars epica: basically the argument for "why" to hear your prayer - describe the nature of the God, your past relationship, and why fulfilling it would be consistent with the God's goals/nature.
- preces: what you're actually asking for.

(More discussion and explanation here: https://axeandplough.com/2016/09/19/prayer-in-a-heathen-context/)

Of course, that structure isn't and shouldn't be the end-all, be-all. As JMG said, communicating and listening to the answers you get is more important than how you do it, but I have found it helpful to have this rough framework to help me get into a prayerful frame of mind.

Anyhow, good luck in your endeavors!
Jef

comments.title: Question about occult studies and bad habits
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Please obliterate this comment/question entry with prejudice if it is either impolite or improper (or both) of me to ask.

It is my speculation that competent occultists tend to be well-organized and not susceptible to hoarding.

And it has been my impression and understanding that your occult studies greatly improved your quality of life.

So I guess my question is, can occult studies conquer/erase/ameliorate annoying habits or bad character defects ... such as being extremely disorganized and being a hoarder?

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: Question about occult studies and bad habits
replies.content:
Yes, but there's a catch. Hoarders aren't hoarders for no reason at all. There are emotional issues they haven't dealt with that lead them to cling to things. Until and unless they deal with those emotional issues, all the magic in the world won't change them.

Now, that being said, one of the things that occult studies will do is make you face your problems and deal with them. Journaling, and especially the focused journaling exercises of the Octagon Society -- one of the traditions I received from my teacher John Gilbert -- will do a lot to further that, and so will the habit of daily meditation. Nonetheless, it's only fair to warn you: it will hurt. Dealing with the unresolved childhood traumas that lead to dysfunctions like hoarding is anything but easy.

If you have that in mind, though, and decide to go through with it, yes, that can help.

replies.title: Follow-up question about hurting
replies.content:
Thank you for your response, and I am always grateful for your Magic Mondays and Monthly Open Posts on the other website :-)
(This thanks, plus the ones I leave after my questions at the bottom of my comments, also and always applies to your responses to my other questions I have asked in the past and will ask in the future. I just don't reply with a thanks every time because I don't want to crowd the comments section)

I do have a follow-up question that was originally meant to be a separate question entry.

I believe I had been avoiding discursive meditation as well as journaling since you first introduced me to the concept on 09-06-2019 on this website (or quickly shutting down after I start) because I simply get too overwhelmed by my thoughts and emotions.

Do you have any suggestions for what to do when thoughts/emotions become too overwhelming, or how not to set myself up for discursive meditation as well as journaling failure? (E.g. Eat a big meal before discursive meditation or journaling?  Journal while listening to music? Pray before discursive meditation or journaling?)

replies.title: Re: Follow-up question about hurting
replies.content: Kimberly's advice is also mine. Don't use discursive meditation on anything emotionally difficult until you're good at it! Get an introductory book on some magical and spiritual path that interests you, and meditate your way through it paragraph by paragraph -- many classic occult books are set up with numbered paragraphs to facilitate that, and I've copied that in my Workbooks. Get comfortable with thinking about something simple, calm, and abstract in a meditative way, and then go on to more complex and challenging things.

You've mentioned before that you have a demon. Have you considered that she might be the one getting you involuntarily enfolded into gunked up egregors? That's exactly the kind of technique that demons on the mental plane tend to exploit. They like to play mind games with their hosts, which often lead to their hosts' minds racing hyperactively in an attempt to stay a step ahead of the demon's dreary antics.

The first thing to do is dress like them. Your whole appearance must be compatible with the group.
The second is respect their rules. Do not differ from what they do.

replies.title: Re: Moving quietly through the masses
replies.content: What I do is similar to what I do when exposed to television: I try to remain reflective, and think about what I am seeing/hearing vs allowing myself to be sucked in experientially. What are the customs and implicit assumptions of this group? What do their mannerisms, clothing, decor, etc indicate about them? How much of that is deliberate vs unconscious on their part? What are the touchstones or shibboleths or in-jokes of the group and what are those things meant to reinforce? Also, I don’t lie, but I do have secrets. Keeping something deliberately secret creates a little insulation between you and others; you must remain somewhat aware and apart to keep a secret. Doesn’t have to be anything major. You could also choose something like never saying a particular word when in that group.  Anything to keep you fully aware instead of sleepwalking.

replies.title: Re: Moving quietly through the masses
replies.content:
Like our host, I'm not neurotypical, but in some other way that doesn't seem to lie on the autism spectrum. (Are there many different ways to be non-neurotypical? I would say, Yes.) I don't have the problem you mention with egregores, since I find it almost intolerable to spend time in large groups of people who are strongly interacting with one another. I can't do teamwork of any sort, for example, or even endure being among members of a working team for any length of time. I'm inherently a solitary human.

As I've mentioned before, I am unable to think using sentences, propositions or narratives. My mental processes are almost completely language-free, though once they are complete, I can certainly concoct sentences and narratives that try to communicate them to other people. But it seems to me that very many (almost all?) egregores are built up using sentences and narratives. In that case, then I may have some sort of inherent neurological immunity to egregores of any sort. Advertising also has no effect on me; it awakens neither desire nor revulsion.

I'm probably not the only one who has such natural immunity to egregores.

Dear JMG,
I have no idea how widespread this is but I had a surprising conversation a few weeks ago with my lovely older sister. This woman has worked at a large Canadian university for over 30 years and has stewed in political correctness until she has become a part of it.

Our conversation was about her desire that the government should start killing people who have wasted their lives by doing drugs and similar. My sister long ago converted to Judaism of the reformed variety, so what she was saying really shocked and surprised me.

I explained to her that what she was thinking was exactly how the Nazi party started out on their killing spree. She said she didn't support racial profiling but just euthanizing people who were wasting their lives.

comments.title: Foreign Gods
comments.content:
Are there any risks with starting to pray to gods from other cultures? I recently learned of an Aztec god that resonated with me.
Since I'm not familiar with the Aztecs as I was wondering if there are any risks (or advice) in starting to pray to this god?

replies.title: Re: Foreign Gods
replies.content: Um, yes. Before you do anything else, you should find out something about the culture that revered the deity, the traditional rituals and customs surrounding the deity, and the deity's myths and stories. That's a matter of basic courtesy, but it's also a matter of self-protection. Some deities can cause homicidal mania in their worshippers; others can cause a cascade of horrible bad luck if they're not approached in the right way, and so on. Deities are smarter and mightier than you are. They're not accessories -- they're persons in their own right, and need to be approached they way you'd approach somebody much richer and more powerful than you!

replies.title: Re: Foreign Gods
replies.content:
I was in Volcano National Park recently and saw Kilauea erupt. This was one of the most powerful experiences of my life, filled with awe and wonder, and at that moment I felt I should give thanks to Pele, the Hawaiian goddess of the volcano. I thanked her for the eruption and everything associated with it: the beauty and majesty of the volcano; the creation of the islands of Hawaii, and for showing herself in all of her glory at a time when I could see it.

I was not planning or expecting to see Kilauea erupt, have not learned anything beyond the basics about her myths and legends, and know very little about the culture of the Native Hawaiians. Do you see any concerns with that kind of prayer?

replies.title: Re: Foreign Gods
replies.content: I don't know enough about her to be sure.

replies.title: Re: Foreign Gods
replies.content:
I felt a sense of amusement more than anything in response, which I take as a good sign.

Having made that impromptu prayer, I suppose it would probably be good form now to start learning more about her, so that I can decide where to go from here.

replies.title: Re: Foreign Gods
replies.content: That strikes me as very sensible.

replies.title: Re: Foreign Gods
replies.content:
I assume one of the biggest is that I might have unwittingly offended her, but I think I was polite and respectful, and prayers of thanks are likely to be safe, and I take the sense of amusement that I got as a good sign I did not offend her.

The other risk I guess might be that I drew her attention, and drawing a god's attention to your life seems like it could be risky: Pele might either take offense to something else I'm doing and whack me over the head until I change, or she might see potential in me she wants to bring out, which may be quite unpleasant if it does not line up with what I want out of life.

Are there other risks you see with such impromptu prayers like this?

replies.title: Re: Peaceful Home Hoodoo
replies.content:
Yes, though you'll need some specialized supplies for it. This is a common enough issue that you can order Peaceful Home oil (also called Happy Home oil) from hoodoo supply houses; you'll need a bottle. Get a blue candle -- preferably a novena candle, the kind that comes in a glass container, like this --

[IMAGE:0]

-- but you can get by with an ordinary blue candle if that's what you can find.

On a Sunday, take a piece of plain brown paper -- part of a shopping bag is fine -- and tear it into a square 4" or so on a side -- all four edges must be torn, not cut. Using a brand new pen, write the name of your family on it seven times, like this:

The Smith Family
The Smith Family
The Smith Family
The Smith Family
The Smith Family
The Smith Family
The Smith Family

Then turn it 90 degrees and write across the words you've already written:

Peaceful Home
Peaceful Home
Peaceful Home
Peaceful Home
Peaceful Home
Peaceful Home
Peaceful Home

Put a drop of Peaceful Home oil in each corner and one in the center. Then fold the paper in half, folding it toward you (to call blessings to you), rotate it 90 degrees, and fold it toward you again.

Now take your candle. Put seven drops of Peaceful Home oil onto it -- that is, put them into the top of the novena candle, or drip them onto the middle of a regular candle and rub it both ways, from the middle to the top and from the middle to the bottom, so the candle is coated. Put the candle upright on the folded paper -- yes, use a candlestick if your candle isn't in a glass container. Light the candle, while saying a prayer for peace in your home. Let it burn all the way down.

(If you don't have a local hoodoo supply house, you might try here.)

comments.content:
TL;DR
Before suggesting ‘Psychic Defence’ or whatever when the October voting opens for 5th Weds, I thought it would be interesting to ask:
What do people actually mean by the term?
The concept seems more suited to a book than a post!
Anyone have any thoughts, ideas or suggestions?

*****

Five quotes and two questions about an idea for a fifth Wednesday post:

"There is an evil quest too, whose disciples seek to serve their lower nature rather than to conquer it, and whose masters show themselves by action or teaching to be monsters."
***
"The weapons of our warfare are not carnal may as truly be said of the practitioners of Black Magic as of the Church."
***
"Fear is the mind-killer..."
***
"The object of life is not to be on the side of the majority, but to escape finding oneself in the ranks of the insane."
***
"...There’s a lot that I can say without giving advantages to people who practice evil magic.”
***

I’ve been dancing around trying to understand this subject for many years and some of the aspects have included things like:

Knowing of the adversary within & without while avoiding debasement and obsession
The true nature and tools of what is called ‘defence’
Mastering fear and other vices
Staying sane in a time of madness
The mundane and esoteric methods

For sure that reads more like a set of section titles for a book not a post!

It occurs to me that in general, ‘the adversary’ could be more likely to be oneself rather than an external actor and that while there are some ‘bad actors’ outside, the biggest form of ‘defence’ is rising beyond the personal lower tendencies. At the same time, a comprehension and recognition of wider risks or threats makes sense e.g. – to riff off of and mangle Sun Tzu:
Know yourself and recognise ‘the other’ as ‘polarity in flow’ - ‘a foil’ as a character who contrasts with another character – at the very least recognise the patterns and modus operandi of forces in this world of polarities.

These days I think psychic self defence may not be the best description to use because it is too easily related to ‘fighting’ when what is actually happening is something much more subtle and not what it first seems. Yet ‘defence’ is an easily understandable word in this realm. For sure it may look like fighting, but the importance is whether it is out of virtue or out of vice.

comments.content:
This may be slightly off-topic, but a writer/indie game dev I've followed has produced a alternate modern occult setting loosely based off the Rider-Waite Deck. Its ideas of magic are more "summon many-limbed monstrosities" and "become a hairless, iron immortal", but there are some interesting ideas.

Basically, the Gods reside in a plane behind the world, comprising the Wood, the Mansus and the Glory. The Wood represents the Dionysian, primal aspects of human instinct, the Mansus represents the logical, Apollonian aspects, and the Glory is basically a shining magical fountain of eternity all the way at the top.

The plane behind the word was ruled by ancient Gods, or "Hours", prior to agricultural society (the Flint, the Wheel, the Tide, the Seven-Coils), that were then usurped by later Hours (the Forge, the Moth, the Grail, the Colonel and the Mother-of-Ants), who influenced the growth of human civilization. Around this time, a Sun-in-Splendour ("SiS", speculated to be card XXI: the World) descends from the Heavens and puts the magical plane to order. The male SiS is in love with the female Forge (card XX: Judgement), but male on female intercourse leads to offspring, and an urge to eat their young a la Cronus, which is a crime. So on the urging of the Grail (card XIV: The Devil), the Forge splits the SiS in 1453 (around the time the Gregorian calendar is adopted).

The two games happen in the early 20th century, between the world wars in game 1: Cultist Simulator, and after WW2 in game 2: Book of Hours. Two things I want to draw attention to:

(1) The two most influential Hours are probably the Forge of Days and the Grail.
(a) the Forge of Days (XX: Judgement) represents relentless progress and destructive change: she "remakes with fire, ends what will not change and ends all nights". In one route, as an occult apostle of an ascended master, you assemble what is effectively a magical nuclear bomb to blow open a way to the Glory for your master to ascend.

(b) the Red Grail (XIV: the Devil) represents birth and motherhood, but also indulgence, excess, hedonism and cannibalism: she "gives life, takes life, and is not sated". Much of the gameplay involves cannibalism, and I won't go int othe gory details.

(2) In Book of Hours, as a librarian that can shape the magical plane, you have the option to replace the current set of Hours and usher in vaguely-described "Gods-from-Steel", where "The Paths of the Sun open both ways… and down those Paths, Glory will flow like mercury, to blossom in its hundred altars."

Further reading is available at this reasonably maintained wiki: https://secret-histories.fandom.com/wiki/Chronicle

JMG and commentariat, do any of the ideas here ring a bell or sound new? For what it's worth, I'm only describing a fraction of the content - there are very many Hours that oppose a new Dawn and would not particularly welcome Gods-from-Steel: I quite like the Velvet and the Elegiast:
https://secret-histories.fandom.com/wiki/The_Velvet

replies.content:
We moved into our house in 1974, 60 years after it was built (in 1914). We still live there 51 years later, happily and harmoniously.

Instinct alone, not anything I had read about house blessings, led me to get a large smoothly rounded, flattish stone about 7 or 8 inches in diameter from a beach, and to chisel the date 1974 on one surface and our initial (M) on the opposite surface, and put it in our back yard, where it later became the corner of a rectangle of similar stones outlining one part of our garden. It seems to have worked well for us all these years.

Stones are stable and mighty things, and many of them are extraordinarily beautiful, too.

For whatever it may be worth, the anthropologist Irving Howell, who spent decades learning about Ojibwa language and culture, noticed that the Ojibwa language distinguishes between living and non-living things in its grammar -- and that stones, in particular, are classed with living things in that language. Curious about this, he asked an Ojibwa elder whether stones really were alive for him. The elder thought for a while, and answered, "Well, not all stones" -- just most was implied. This led Howell to coin the very useful technical term, now widely used by anthropologists, "other-than-human-persons."

One can learn a great deal about magic from the studies of a various anthropologists who would never think of themselves as occultists. I have learned as much from them, perhaps more, than from all the occultists whom I have read.

comments.title: Energetic anatomy concept and homeopathy
comments.content: Hi JMG, I've recently become very interested in homeopathy and know you have some familiarity with this topic (e.g., from your blog post on the subject). replies.title: Re: Energetic anatomy concept and homeopathy

comments.content:
This will be a longer post containing some reflections and some questions. Thank you in advance for your answers.

For some time now, I’ve been meditating on the role of the magician in today’s world. I understand the need for the development of one’s own spirit and soul, yet what troubles me is: what can we, as magicians/practitioners of occultism and mysticism, actually offer to modern society? Doctors strive to save lives, scientists keep pushing the boundaries of our understanding of the material world—even a plumber seems more “useful” than a magician in our times. So my first question is: What can we, as magicians or practitioners of spell-based magic, really contribute to the world?

The second matter: I recently listened to your guest appearance on the Cunning Plant podcast—fantastic work. I do have some technical questions, though. What technique did you use to “awaken” the statues of the Gods? And do you know of any books on Egyptian magic? (I am particularly interested in the ritual of the Opening of the Mouth.)

The third matter: I love gothic themes, so naturally I’ve explored vampirism and lycanthropy/werewolfs in magic, and I’ve come to the conclusion that both are greatly overrated. Vampirism literally sounds like a curse that forces the practitioner to feed on external energy, and because of this curse, certain abilities become more developed (such as densifying the etheric body, astral projection, or forms of glamour and illusion).
All of these skills can be mastered by a magician without becoming a vampire. The only real “advantage” seems to be delaying the second death, but even then, I feel there are far better—and far more ethical—ways to achieve that.

What truly interests me, though, is this: what are the advantages of becoming a werewolf? I understand the usefulness of learning how to densify one’s etheric or astral body, but why would someone in the 21st century even want this ability? I can see how such techniques might be useful somewhere in Siberia or in cultures closely tied to nature, but why would, say, a city-dweller need the ability to change into an animal?
One last point: Is there any difference between a werewolf and a shapeshifter in general?

comments.title: Genius Loci: Follow-up Questions.
comments.content:
Further to my query last week, thanks for confirming my sensations about different places. Years ago, you discussed how the spirit of a place changes the people, but do the people also change the spirit of the place as well over time?
Paris, 2000-odd years ago, was a minor fort on an island called Lutetia of the minor Parisii tribe. I was reflecting on how, before the Muslim conquest of North Africa, it was a lush place, and recall a remark that the Muslim destruction of the ancient temples may have played a part in the subsequent desertification. I vaguely recall something about the ancient temples as constructed increased microvoltage in the soil, and hence crop yields? And wasn't there something about ancient Mesopotamia having salted up more than once in the past and the ancient people remedied it, but the current salinity happened in the 13th or 14th century and thus the land yield has dramatically decreased but no one, this time, has done anything about it. Which would suggest that something in the place has changed in the past thousand years to make it different from the previous 5000.

At any rate, I'm happy to be back and dealing with the energies I'm used to.

Bruce (Renaissance Man)

replies.title: Re: Genius Loci: Follow-up Questions.
replies.content: The relation between the spirit of the place and the spirit of the people is complex, and I don't know if anyone human knows exactly how that works. As for the temple technology, though, that's the subject of my soon-to-be-reprinted book The Secret of the Temple. While the exact mechanism is still unclear, yes, ancient temples had a positive effect on crop yields, as described by many ancient testimonies; the Temple of Solomon had that effect, as the Talmud explains in detail. Medieval churches in the west of Europe ended up being built using the same archaic folk technology, and had that effect; Orthodox churches weren't and didn't, and mosques don't have it at all -- Islam drew a very hard line against borrowings from the older Pagan faiths, and so the temple technology stopped being used where Islam spread, with results that can be seen quite readily.

replies.title: Re: Genius Loci: Follow-up Questions.
replies.content: If Islam takes over Western Europe, I wonder if they would end up repurposing the churches in Western Europe for their mosques or if they would tear down the churches and build their own mosques. Because if the latter I then worry that Western Europe's agriculture will suffer under Islamic rule.

replies.title: Re: Genius Loci: Follow-up Questions.
replies.content: The temple technology is at most faltering there anyway -- it's not just the buildings, there are particular kinds of work that have to be done there, and those haven't been done reliably since the Reformation and Counter-Reformation. Look up how many villages in Britain were abandoned once the monasteries were abolished under Henry VIII -- it's quite a remarkable toll.

replies.title: Re: Genius Loci: Follow-up Questions.
replies.content: FWIW, both North Africa and Mesopotamia were badly damaged by nomadic incursions: in the case of North Africa, a confederation on Bedouin tribes called the Banu Hilal conquered much of the region around the year 1000, and are recorded to have burned farms and destroyed irrigation infrastructure in an effort to make the region more hospitable to their nomadic, animal raising way of life. Mesopotamia likewise was invaded by the Mongols in the 13th century, and the Mongols were frankly the closest thing the pre-modern world had to a nuclear bomb. Historical records indicate that most cities in modern day Iraq, and a lot of the intricate irrigation systems that had existed since ancient times, were simply destroyed, and likewise the modern day Arabic dialect spoken in Baghdad and all of Iraq south of Baghdad is closer to that of the Gulf States than to the dialect of pre-Mongol Iraq (indicating that the original Iraqi population was largely wiped out and later replaced with immigrants from the south).

replies.title: Re: Genius Loci: Follow-up Questions.
replies.content: Fair enough. One of the other contributing factors is that malefic astrological magic was much practiced in medieval Muslim culture, and some sources known to me include methods for making a talisman to depopulate and destroy an entire city. Now of course magic doesn't do anything with a flash and a bang; attracting nomad armies, and making them more than usually destructive, would be well within the range of possibility, though.

comments.title: Sharing unusual experience
comments.content:
Good afternoon, JMG!

I don't really have a question today, but I'd like to share a strange thing that happened to me today.

I wear vests daily as part of my daily office wear (it's too hot for jackets around here). I cycle through three vests normally, with different shirt combinations underneath. Recently, one of my vests (a wool one from Ireland) went missing for three weeks. I found it again last Friday, hanging up in my closet where it belongs, in an area I definitely searched several times. Celebrating the return of this vest, I wore it to church on Sunday, and prepped it to wear to work on Monday. This morning, I picked it up from the staging area and into my closet to change without waking anyone, but on that short trip the vest had disappeared! I spent as long as I could looking for it, but had to settle on a different combination so as not to miss work. I also had to rescue a small lizard who fell in my sink last night.

When I returned from work this afternoon, my vest was hanging on the mailbox - somehow the lawn service found it outside in the yard, somewhere I definitely didn't go this morning.

I hadn't thought of suburban Florida as a place for dynamic fey activity, but there does seem to be something funny going on!

replies.title: Re: Sharing unusual experience
replies.content: How very odd. It makes a certain quirky sense, though, that the local fey would want to borrow a vest from Ireland!

comments.title: Alienation
comments.content:
One last question for this week: Do languages have egregores and can these assert themselves inside of a person? Most of my thoughts switched to English in my mid to late teens. My personal notes are in English, as are most of the books I read and online forums that I participate in. Culturally too, I find both America and Britain, different though they are, much more appealing than my native Germany—this may well be a case of the grass always being greener on the other side of the fence, though.

And yet, every now and then, I get the distinct feeling that I should be thinking, writing, and doing rituals¹ in German. Even with today's decision to switch to a paper practise record, something inside of me piped up and said that if I'm learning shorthand for this, I might as well just switch language entirely and learn a German shorthand system. And yet, a couple of hours afterwards, my thoughts have reverted back to English and it would feel odd not to use the language my mind apparently operates in. But isn't that weird? I'm not just talking about any foreign language; this is my mother tongue.

Thank you in advance for your answer, as I'll hopefully be fast asleep by the time you post it.

—David P.

1: One of my very first attempts at a ritual featured a miserable attempt at translating on the fly. I burned myself on an incense stick and my cat briefly went missing during a thunderstorm that started the moment I was done. Fun times.

replies.title: Re: Alienation
replies.content: Languages certainly have egregors. Whether they can assert themselves inside an individual -- hmm. I don't know of any source that discusses that, but it's at least possible.

replies.title: Languages and their Egregores
replies.content:
Professional linguists, so far as I know, have never used the term egregore in their published work, even if a few of them have also been occultists.

But there are some anthropological linguists who have played with the concept under other names, mostly the German term Weltanschauung, "world-view." Among them was Benjamin Lee Whorf, who was a life-long occultist and eventually also became a member of the Theosophical Society. (His Selected Writings(1956) can be found and downloaded here and there on the web, and his two most relevant papers have the titles "The Relation of Habitual Thought and Behavior to Language"and "Language, Mind and Reality." I have also posted the original publications of them on archive.org.)

More recently a fair amount of significant work on the different Weltanschauungen that are embodied in different languages (and about Whorf's own work) has been published by such linguists and anthropologists as Dan Moonhawk Alford, Peter C. Rollins, Jason Ānanda Josephson Storm, John E. Joseph, and Penny Lee. And the list of these publications is still growing ...

All this, naturally, has proved unpalatable to mainstream materialist science and philosophy, which has produced a fair number of pseudo-refutations to it; all (or rather, the ones I have read) of them seem to me to rest on overly simplistic assumptions about the ways in which humans necessarily perceive and conceptualize reality.

I certainly agree with our host that each language, and thus each language community, has its own egregore.

replies.title: Re: Alienation
replies.content:
An alternative hypothesis: this being your native tongue is an important detail. It could well be some sort of ancestor's influence.

I can offer a vaguely related data point. After I reached a certain level of proficiency in my father's native tongue, I began to have occasional dreams where a long-gone elderly aunt offers excellent advice in that language. Conversely, I never had anything like that with the foreign language I studied in school, even when I was working with it at a comparable level.

comments.title: The Watch
comments.content:
I have been mulling something over for sometime now. I started thinking about it when a weird commenter suggested on the main site that they had started the big fire in Hawaii magically. This reminded me of a passage by Dion Fortune suggesting there was a group of beings that noted and regulated these kinds of practices, kind of like magical police. Also a very clear image of a particular type of hound dog called a Harrier Hound, bred to hunt rabbits, became prominent in my mind. We bred it for that purpose specifically. If you happen to have one on a leash and a rabbit is flushed out before you the dog will pursue with a wildness and furiosity, displaying amazing strength.
Is this a way councils of occultists police magic? There must be checks... or another inquisition starts. Do creatures exist on the planes, bred specifically to pursue sorcerers who step far off the path, creatures awakened at the scent of despicable magic? Or do these traits exist hidden in some humans? Do we become the hounds that pursue such wickedness when awakened? This commenter was immensely stupid to bound out in public.

replies.title: Re: The Watch
replies.content:
For a while, in Britain and a few other countries, there was an organized network of lodges that policed the magical community. They weren't everywhere -- the Ariosophist groups that gave rise to Nazism would never have gotten going, for example, if the old "hunting lodges" had been active in Germany or Austria -- but where they were active, they had some ability to stop the worst kind of abuses, partly via magic, partly via well-timed press releases and leaks, and partly via friendly relations with certain people at Scotland Yard.

That system broke down after the Second World War, when magical lodges of what had been the standard type became utterly unfashionable, the media started encouraging grubby behavior rather than exposing it, and popular culture embraced and glorified transgressive activity. It was always a network of ordinary human occultists, though it had inner plane beings who worked with it, and so it came apart as so many of the old lodges did.

That's profoundly regrettable at this point, as a network like that might have been able to do something to stop the frightful moral collapse of the Neopagan scene. As it is, it may be necessary for something of the sort to be reconstituted, slowly and carefully, with much attention to weeding out sleeper agents from the other side.

replies.title: Re: The Watch
replies.content: Do you have any idea why so many things went so off the rails after the Second World War?

replies.title: Re: The Watch
replies.content:
It's a very complex matter. The very, very short form is that European civilization peaked in the decades before 1914 and began its decline thereafter; the 1920s and 1930s saw rot set in, and thereafter it just got steadily worse, not least because so many people were convinced that whatever was new was progressive, and therefore good. I think of a passage from one of my favorite Robinson Jeffers poems:

"You making haste haste on decay: not blameworthy; life is good, be it stubbornly long or suddenly
A mortal splendor: meteors are not needed less than mountains: shine, perishing republic."

replies.title: Re: The Watch
replies.content: I will study the available material.

replies.title: Re: House cleansing
